findings.title: AC15: Hardcoded password hash for 'root' directly in passwd (read-only)
findings.details: The passwd file contains a DES crypt hash directly (not shadowed) for user 'root' (uid=0, shell=/bin/sh). Hash: $1$nalENqL8$jnRFwb1x5S.ygN.3nwTbG1. DES crypt is extremely weak — only 8 character max password length, crackable in seconds. The user has shell access (/bin/sh) and uid=0.

findings.title: AC15: Hardcoded password hash for 'user' directly in passwd (read-only)

findings.title: AC15: Hidden root-level account 'user' (uid=0) in passwd (read-only)
findings.details: The passwd file contains a hidden root-equivalent account 'user' with uid=0. Full line: user:tGqcT.qjxbEik:0:0:Normal User:/:/bin/sh

findings.title: AC15: Hardcoded password hash for 'nobody' directly in passwd (read-only)
findings.details: The passwd file contains a DES crypt hash directly (not shadowed) for user 'nobody' (uid=0, shell=/bin/sh). Hash: VBcCXSNG7zBAY. DES crypt is extremely weak — only 8 character max password length, crackable in seconds. The user has shell access (/bin/sh) and uid=0.

findings.title: AC15: Hidden root-level account 'nobody' (uid=0) in passwd (read-only)
findings.details: The passwd file contains a hidden root-equivalent account 'nobody' with uid=0. Full line: nobody:VBcCXSNG7zBAY:0:0:nobody for ftp:/:/bin/sh

findings.title: AC15: Hardcoded MD5 crypt password hash for 'root' in shadow (read-only)
findings.details: The firmware ships with a pre-computed password hash for user 'root' in etc_ro/shadow. Hash: $1$OVhtCyFa$7tISyKW1KGssHAQj1vI3i1 (type: MD5 crypt). This password is the same across all devices of this model, enabling trivial offline cracking and lateral movement.

findings.title: AC15: Password stored in HTTP cookie
findings.details: The httpd binary contains format string 'Set-Cookie: password=%s; path=/' indicating the admin password is stored in a plaintext HTTP cookie. This exposes credentials to XSS, network sniffing, and cookie theft. Matches: ['Set-Cookie: password=%s; path=/']

findings.title: AC15: Hidden telnet daemon activation via /goform/telnet
findings.details: The httpd binary contains code to start telnetd: ['telnetd -b %s &']. Combined with the /goform/telnet endpoint and hardcoded root password hashes, this provides unauthenticated remote root shell access. The 'load telnetd success.' string confirms the feature exists.

findings.title: AC15: Hardcoded RSA Private Key shipped in firmware at /webroot_ro/pem/privkeySrv.pem
findings.details: The firmware ships with a static private key at /webroot_ro/pem/privkeySrv.pem (SHA-256: a2fc92558d8f08a9503c49ffedb9ea8798f569d3a783df43e2715913f7ad99f0). This key is identical across ALL devices of this model, enabling: (1) TLS MITM attacks against any device using HTTPS, (2) impersonation of any device, (3) decryption of captured HTTPS traffic. The same private key was found in both AC15 and AC20 firmware, indicating cross-model key reuse.

findings.title: AC15: Hardcoded default service passwords in config files
findings.details: The firmware ships with hardcoded default passwords for multiple services. Passwords: [sys.baseuserpass=user (/webroot_ro/default.cfg); usb.ftp.pwd=admin (/webroot_ro/default.cfg); usb.samba.pwd=admin (/webroot_ro/default.cfg); usb.samba.guest.pwd=guest (/webroot_ro/default.cfg)]. Usernames: [sys.baseusername=user (/webroot_ro/default.cfg); sys.baseuserpass=user (/webroot_ro/default.cfg); sys.username=admin (/webroot_ro/default.cfg); usb.ftp.user=admin (/webroot_ro/default.cfg); usb.samba.user=admin (/webroot_ro/default.cfg); usb.samba.guest.user=guest (/webroot_ro/default.cfg)]. These defaults are well-known and documented, providing trivial access to FTP, Samba, and admin interfaces.

findings.title: AC15: Static WPS PIN hardcoded in configuration
findings.details: The firmware ships with a static WPS PIN value that is the same across all devices: [wl2g.public.wps_ap_pin=16677883 (/webroot_ro/default.cfg); wl5g.public.wps_ap_pin=16677883 (/webroot_ro/default.cfg)]. A static WPS PIN enables offline brute-force attacks and Pixie Dust attacks.

findings.title: AC15: Critical hidden/undocumented goform endpoints
findings.details: The httpd binary contains 3 critical endpoints not referenced in the web UI: ['telnet', 'ate', 'formMfgTest']. Particularly dangerous: /goform/telnet (starts telnetd for remote shell), TendaConsoleSwitchOpen/Close (enables debug console), /goform/ate (manufacturing test mode with full system access). These endpoints may be accessible without authentication.

findings.title: AC15: Telnet daemon can be remotely activated via HTTP
findings.details: The httpd binary contains functionality to start telnetd via HTTP request. goform/telnet endpoint exists: True. telnetd launch command: True. Console switch default: None. Combined with hardcoded root credentials in /etc_ro/passwd and /etc_ro/shadow, this allows complete remote device takeover via: (1) POST to /goform/telnet, (2) telnet to device with root credentials.

findings.title: AC15: Manufacturing test (ATE) functions accessible in production firmware
findings.details: The httpd binary contains 22 ATE (Automated Test Equipment) functions that should be disabled in production firmware. These include: ['Set_Mfg_Button_Test', 'TendaAte', 'ate_Tenda_mfg_LanWanInfo', 'ate_Tenda_mfg_check_usb', 'ate_Tenda_mfg_check_usb3', 'ate_Tenda_mfg_default', 'ate_Tenda_mfg_reboot', 'ate_envram_commit_common', 'ate_envram_set_misc', 'ate_main_handle']. ATE functions typically allow: MAC address manipulation, NVRAM read/write, device reboot, radio calibration, and factory reset — all without authentication.

findings.title: AC15: Known backdoor patterns found in firmware
findings.details: Found 1 known backdoor patterns: ASUS: Known ASUS backdoor reference

findings.title: AC15: Default admin password is EMPTY
findings.details: The NVRAM default configuration sets sys.userpass= (empty string), meaning the admin web interface has NO password by default. Combined with sys.username=admin, any user on the network can access the full admin interface without authentication. File: /webroot_ro/default.cfg

findings.title: AC15: FTP enabled by default with default credentials
findings.details: FTP service is enabled by default (usb.ftp.enable=1) with credentials usb.ftp.user=admin, usb.ftp.pwd=admin. Anonymous FTP is also configured in vsftpd.conf (anonymous_enable=YES).

findings.title: AC15: Default WEP keys present (weak cryptography)
findings.details: Default WEP keys are configured: wl2g.ssid0.wep_key1=12345; wl5g.ssid0.wep_key1=12345. WEP encryption is cryptographically broken and can be cracked in minutes.

findings.title: AC15: Samba configured to accept null (empty) passwords
findings.details: The smb.conf contains 'null passwords = yes', allowing connections with empty passwords. Combined with the hardcoded admin credentials, this provides trivial access to shared USB storage.

findings.title: AC15: vsftpd configured with anonymous access enabled
findings.details: The vsftpd.conf enables anonymous FTP access (anonymous_enable=YES). Combined with the FTP service being enabled by default in NVRAM (usb.ftp.enable=1), this provides unauthenticated read access to USB-connected storage.

findings.title: AC20: Hardcoded password hash for 'root' directly in passwd (read-only)
findings.details: The passwd file contains a DES crypt hash directly (not shadowed) for user 'root' (uid=0, shell=/bin/sh). Hash: $1$nalENqL8$jnRFwb1x5S.ygN.3nwTbG1. DES crypt is extremely weak — only 8 character max password length, crackable in seconds. The user has shell access (/bin/sh) and uid=0.

findings.details: The firmware ships with a static private key at /webroot_ro/pem/privkeySrv.pem (SHA-256: a2fc92558d8f08a9503c49ffedb9ea8798f569d3a783df43e2715913f7ad99f0). Usernames: [sys.baseusername=user (/webroot_ro/default.cfg); sys.baseuserpass=user (/webroot_ro/default.cfg); sys.username=admin (/webroot_ro/default.cfg); usb.ftp.user=admin (/webroot_ro/default.cfg); usb.samba.user=admin (/webroot_ro/default.cfg); usb.samba.guest.user=guest1 (/webroot_ro/default.cfg)]. Particularly dangerous: /goform/telnet (starts telnetd for remote shell), TendaConsoleSwitchOpen/Close (enables debug console), /goform/ate (manufacturing test mode with full system access). findings.details: The httpd binary contains functionality to start telnetd via HTTP request. goform/telnet endpoint exists: True. telnetd launch command: True. Console switch default: console_switch=disable. Combined with hardcoded root credentials in /etc_ro/passwd and /etc_ro/shadow, this allows complete remote device takeover via: (1) POST to /goform/telnet, (2) telnet to device with root credentials.

findings.title: AC20: Manufacturing test (ATE) functions accessible in production firmware
findings.details: The httpd binary contains 1 ATE (Automated Test Equipment) functions that should be disabled in production firmware. These include: ['TendaAte']. ATE functions typically allow: MAC address manipulation, NVRAM read/write, device reboot, radio calibration, and factory reset — all without authentication.

findings.title: AC20: Default admin password is EMPTY
findings.details: The NVRAM default configuration sets sys.userpass= (empty string), meaning the admin web interface has NO password by default. Combined with sys.username=admin, any user on the network can access the full admin interface without authentication. File: /webroot_ro/default.cfg

findings.title: AC20: WiFi SSIDs configured with NO encryption by default
findings.details: The following WiFi interfaces default to no security (open/none): wlan0.0_bss_security=none; wlan1.0_bss_security=none. All traffic on these networks is unencrypted and susceptible to eavesdropping and MITM attacks.

findings.title: AC20: FTP enabled by default with default credentials
findings.details: FTP service is enabled by default (usb.ftp.enable=1) with credentials usb.ftp.user=admin, usb.ftp.pwd=admin. Anonymous FTP is also configured in vsftpd.conf (anonymous_enable=YES).

findings.title: AC20: vsftpd configured with anonymous access enabled
findings.details: The vsftpd.conf enables anonymous FTP access (anonymous_enable=YES). Combined with the FTP service being enabled by default in NVRAM (usb.ftp.enable=1), this provides unauthenticated read access to USB-connected storage.

findings.title: AC15 and AC20 share the SAME hardcoded TLS private key
findings.details: Both the AC15 (ARM) and AC20 (MIPS) firmware images contain the identical RSA private key for HTTPS. This means: (1) compromising one model's TLS key compromises ALL Tenda devices using this key, (2) the key is publicly available in firmware images on GitHub, (3) any attacker can perform MITM attacks on HTTPS connections to ANY device running either firmware version.

findings.title: AC15 and AC20 share the SAME root password hash
findings.details: Both models use identical root password hash in /etc_ro/shadow: root:$1$OVhtCyFa$7tISyKW1KGssHAQj1vI3i1:14319::::::. This is an MD5 crypt hash ($1$) that is shared across model lines, meaning cracking it once provides root access to all affected devices.

findings.title: AC15 has 4 additional root-level accounts not in AC20
findings.details: The AC15 firmware contains extra accounts: {'support', 'nobody', 'admin', 'user'}. All have uid=0 (root equivalent) with DES crypt password hashes and /bin/sh shell access. Accounts: admin, support, user, nobody — all with uid=0. These represent multiple hardcoded backdoor accounts.
